_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
condition: Pulmonary Atresia with Ventricular Septal Defect and Major Aortopulmonary Collateral Arteries (MAPCA)
procedure_name: Unifocalization of Major Aortopulmonary Collateral Arteries (MAPCA)
clinical_indications: Ventricular septal defect with tricuspid atresia- Type C and D.
Type C - Long-segment pulmonary atresia with absent main pulmonary artery. Branch pulmonary arteries confluent, but pulmonary blood flow dependent predominantly on MAPCAs.
Type D - Long-segment pulmonary atresia with absent main pulmonary artery. Nonconfluent branch pulmonary arteries with MAPCA-dependent pulmonary blood flow.

claim.label: Post procedure stills of ECHO with report

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes include detailed history, signs & symptoms, and indication for procedure?
Echo/Doppler report is suggestive of Major aorto-pulmonary collateral arteries?
CT Angio/Cardiac Catheterization report (if available) is suggestive of Major aorto-pulmonary collateral arteries?

cpd_claim.q: Post-procedure ECHO shows repair of the defect?
Detailed Discharge Summary with follow-up advice available?

common_queries: Echo/Doppler report does not indicate Major aorto-pulmonary collateral arteries.
Clinical notes are missing or lack detailed history, signs, symptoms, or indication for procedure.
CT Angio/Cardiac Catheterization report (if available) is missing or not suggestive of Major aorto-pulmonary collateral arteries.
Detailed Procedure / Operative notes are missing.
Post-procedure ECHO report does not demonstrate repair of the defect.
Detailed Discharge Summary is missing or lacks follow-up advice.

clinical_key_pointers: Treatment of pulmonary atresia with ventricular septal defect and major aortopulmonary collateral arteries is challenging and controversial.
Basically, the collateral arteries are treated surgically by unifocalization to integrate them and unify the blood flow in the pulmonary circulation.
These major collateral arteries are anatomically similar to the bronchial arteries; however, they develop into different vessels because of different environmental exposures.

Currently, treatment plans involving surgical intervention in early infancy to address the multiple variations of major aortopulmonary collateral arteries are being established to achieve definitive intracardiac repair.

Historically, several surgical approaches have been proposed, including multi-stage unifocalization performed by lateral thoracotomy, rehabilitation of the central pulmonary artery followed by intracardiac repair, and single-stage unifocalization by a midline incision.
Recently, single-stage unifocalization performed during infancy has become the preferred method for achieving low right ventricular pressure after closing the ventricular septal defect.
Furthermore, for the maintenance of the lowest possible right ventricular pressure after definitive repair, combination therapy or hybrid therapy with catheter and surgical intervention is essential.

Diagnostic Workup includes: Clinical assessment, Pulse Oximetry (measuring oxygen saturation, noting anemia's effect on cyanosis), X-ray Chest (looking for cardiomegaly/pulmonary oligemia, heterogeneous reticular appearance in MAPCA-dependent flow, right-sided aortic arch prevalence), ECG (right-axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy, biventricular hypertrophy/left atrial enlargement with increased pulmonary blood flow).
Echocardiography is vital for diagnosis but may not delineate distal pulmonary arterial tree; additional imaging (cardiac catheterization, CTA/CMRI) is essential for planning definitive repair.

Cardiac catheterization is almost always performed in patients with VSD-PAt before definitive repair, especially after prior aortopulmonary shunt surgery (Class I for Type C/D, Class IIa for Type A/B). It helps assess shunt patency, pulmonary artery branch confluence, pressure, MAPCA distribution/size, recruitable lung segments, and single/dual lung segment supply, and operability in late-presenting large MAPCAs.
CTA is a vital investigation for planning surgery, recommended for all patients planned for repair (Class I), providing clear definition of lung segments supplied by native pulmonary arteries.
surgical_options: Multi-stage unifocalization performed by lateral thoracotomy.
Rehabilitation of the central pulmonary artery followed by intracardiac repair.
Single-stage unifocalization by a midline incision.
Single-stage unifocalization performed during infancy (preferred method).
